title: Bridge to Infinity — Integrated cislunar logistics model
description: Portable parameter pack for the Bridge to Infinity architecture: electromagnetic launcher, aerodynamic turn, lunar export, habitat completion, and matched-flow population model. Not a forecast — illustrative scenario from the essay thesis.
source: Age of Wonders essay: Bridge to Infinity (Appendices B–D, C2, L, M)

reference_design.description: Primary design point used throughout the essay. 8 km summit + 12 km tension-stabilised CNT pylon = 20 km near-vertical exit. Cargo exits at 3 km/s; human flights exit at 700 m/s. Both share the same pylon and exit altitude; tunnel velocity, tunnel length, and bank program differ. Both tunnels are ~30 km.

g_philosophy.cargo: 20g sustained throughout linear tunnel (~15 s) + 30g curvilinear fine alignment (~2 s) + 20g aero bank (full C_L, <3 s)
g_philosophy.human: 3g sustained linear acceleration in tunnel (~29 s to 852 m/s) + 3–7g centripetal in curvilinear section (~21 s, coils maintain velocity only, 7g for <3 s) + coast through pylon (~16 s, 852→700 m/s gravity loss) + ~1g aero bank at full C_L (~11 s) + engine burn ~0.9g→5g over ~420 s (85% below 3g). Total tunnel entry to orbit: ~8 minutes.
g_philosophy.rationale: At 852 m/s summit velocity, the curvilinear turn radius is ~15 km at 5g — geometrically feasible within a mountain. Exit dynamic pressure is 22 kPa, below rocket Max Q. The vehicle banks at full authority with only ~1g peak. Both tunnels are ~30 km (cargo at 20g: ~28 km; human at 3g: ~30 km). The propellant penalty is modest: 58% savings vs 64% for cargo. Human savings come mainly from loss elimination, not aerodynamics.

human_summary.tunnel_profile: 12 km at 3g sustained (linear acceleration to 852 m/s) + 18 km curvilinear (3–7g centripetal, 7g for <3 s, coils maintain velocity only)
human_summary.aero_note: At 700 m/s, full C_L = 0.5 produces ~1g peak — the bank is barely perceptible. Human savings come from loss elimination (1.3 km/s), not the aero turn (~105 m/s).
human_summary.vehicle_type: Near-vacuum optimised space-plane. Exits at Mach 2.4 from 20 km, banks aerodynamically, uses wing lift to maintain altitude during engine burn. Structurally simpler than the cargo lifting body — exit dynamic pressure (22 kPa) is gentler than rocket Max Q.

reference_design.rationale_exit_velocity_cargo: Aero Δv scales linearly with v₀; launcher energy and tunnel length scale as v₀². At 3 km/s the marginal propellant saving is diminishing while infrastructure costs accelerate — the knee of the curve.
reference_design.rationale_exit_velocity_human: At 3 km/s, the curvilinear turn radius at 5g is 184 km — geometrically impossible within any mountain. At 852 m/s summit velocity (700 m/s exit after pylon coast), r = 15 km — feasible. The lower speed reduces exit dynamic pressure from 401 kPa to 22 kPa, gives a ~30 km tunnel (matching cargo scale), and the propellant penalty (58% vs 64% savings) is modest. Human savings come mainly from loss elimination (1.3 km/s), not the aero turn.
reference_design.rationale_exit_altitude: Lower exit = more air = more aero Δv. The pylon is the binding constraint, not the aerodynamics. 20 km from an 8 km summit with a 12 km pylon is the practical sweet spot.
reference_design.rationale_near_vertical: Tension-stabilised vertical mast is the simplest stayed geometry (symmetric guys, pure compression into mountain). Both cargo and human vehicles share the same pylon — tunnel velocity, tunnel length, and bank program differ.
reference_design.pylon_coasting_note: The pylon contains no accelerating coils. All acceleration is completed inside the mountain tunnel before the payload reaches the summit. Cargo at 3,039 m/s loses ~39 m/s over ~4 s (negligible). Human vehicle at 852 m/s loses ~152 m/s over ~16 s, arriving at 700 m/s — significant, but pre-loaded by the tunnel. The pylon carries only its own weight, vacuum containment, and wind loading — never the reaction forces of accelerating a vehicle.

launcher_architecture.gravity_loss_note: Fully eliminated. Vertical climb is electromagnetic (no engine burn). Vehicle banks aerodynamically and ignites near-horizontal. No powered flight against gravity vector.

shared_bore.note: One bore serves both vehicle classes. 12 km linear + 18 km curvilinear (clothoid, 70° turn toward vertical). Humans accelerate at 3g in the linear section, 5-7g centripetal in the curve. Cargo accelerates at 20+g throughout, reaching up to 3 km/s — the curve produces 30-50g centripetal at cargo speeds.

cargo_tunnel.note: Cargo accelerates at 20+g through the full 30 km shared bore (linear + curvilinear). Centripetal forces in the curve reach 30-50g at cargo speeds — within ruggedised tolerances.

curvilinear_section.purpose: Curves trajectory from bore angle to near-vertical for pylon entry
curvilinear_section.coils: maintain velocity against gravity only — no linear acceleration

human_rated_tunnel.note: 12 km at 3g sustained (linear acceleration to 852 m/s) + 18 km curvilinear (3–7g centripetal, 7g <3 s). At 852 m/s the turn radius is 15 km at 5g — geometrically feasible (vs 184 km at 3 km/s). Same pylon as cargo. The 16 s pylon coast loses 152 m/s to gravity, arriving at 700 m/s at the top. Both tunnels are ~30 km.

aerodynamic_turn.scaling_law: Every 7.5 km lower in exit altitude doubles Δv_aero
aerodynamic_turn.peak_g_formula: a_peak = (ρ₀ × v₀² × S × C_L) / (2m), decays to 37% in H/v₀ ≈ 2.5 seconds
aerodynamic_turn.wing_loading_formula: Δv_aero = ρ₀ × v₀ × H × C_L × g / (2 × R × W_L). Absolute mass cancels: planform S is set by landing wing loading W_L = m_final × g / S, and m_exit = R × m_final.

cargo_purpose_built.note: S/m ≈ 0.001 m²/kg — mass cancels in closed-form via wing loading constraint

cargo_conservative.note: Smaller planform, moderate bank. S/m set by W_L.

exit_20km_human_700ms_full_bank.note: Same pylon, same exit altitude, different exit velocity. At 700 m/s, full C_L produces ~1g peak — barely perceptible. Human savings come from loss elimination, not the aero turn.

aerodynamic_turn.drag_penalty: At L/D = 2, drag costs ~half the lift impulse from vertical velocity. Deducted from surplus vertical budget (vehicle has ~3000 m/s, needs only ~500-700 m/s residual). Never the binding constraint.

scenarios.label: Launcher + aero (20 km human 700 m/s) + hydrolox

energy_budget.dry_fraction_sensitivity: Moving from 10% to 15% dry fraction roughly doubles total energy per kg payload.

three_layers_of_free_delta_v: 1. Electricity → 700–3000 m/s vertical (eliminates drag, gravity loss, steering loss: ~1.3–1.5 km/s). Cargo at 3 km/s; humans at 700 m/s.
2. Aerodynamics → 105–500 m/s horizontal (lifting body banks in thin air, converts surplus vertical: zero propellant cost). Cargo at 20g peak; humans at ~1g full bank.
3. Vacuum → engine burns near-horizontal with head start from layers 1+2. Cargo 7.2 km/s duty; human 7.8 km/s duty.

engine_burn_physics.note: Constant-thrust engine: acceleration increases as propellant is consumed. a_final / a_initial = mass_ratio.
engine_burn_physics.formula_burn_time: t_burn = mf × (MR - 1) × v_e / T, where T = mf × a_final
engine_burn_physics.formula_g_at_time: a(t) = T / (m₀ - ṁt), smoothly increasing from a_initial to a_final
engine_burn_physics.burn_window_note: The ~10 min unpowered ballistic window is only a constraint for the first 1-2 minutes. Once the engine has built several km/s of horizontal velocity, the vehicle is partially in orbit and no longer needs the ballistic arc to stay above the atmosphere.

human_launch_profile.description: Complete timeline from tunnel entry to orbit for human-rated vehicle at reference design (20 km exit, 700 m/s).

phases.feel: Firm push into seat back. Half a minute.
Progressive roller-coaster banked turn through the mountain's shoulder
Sixteen seconds of silence. Vacuum. Stars visible. Earth's curve on the horizon.
Barely perceptible roll. Dynamic pressure 22 kPa, gentler than rocket Max Q.
Gradually building, comfortable. Wing lift maintains altitude in thin air.
Strong push, brief — final propellant burning off

human_launch_profile.key_insight: The harshest moment is the tunnel curve (7g for <3 seconds at the tightest bend). The aero bank is barely perceptible (~1g). The engine burn starts gently (~0.9g) and builds to 5g final over ~7 minutes; about 85% of the burn is below 3g. Total tunnel to orbit: ~8 minutes. This is a transit system with one sharp bend in the mountain.

shared_infrastructure_principle: One ~30 km bore (12 km linear + 18 km curvilinear toward vertical) serves both cargo and human vehicles. The same coils, the same vacuum, the same mountain. Humans accelerate at 3g, navigating the curve at 5-7g centripetal. Cargo accelerates at 20+g throughout, reaching up to 3 km/s — the curve that gives humans a roller-coaster gives cargo 30-50g. Both exit through the same pylon (coasting section, 20 km). One tunnel. One pylon. Two programs.

two_stage_decay_model.phase_1: Bootstrap: 1.5 t/person, decaying ~3%/yr as orbital fabs come online
two_stage_decay_model.phase_2: Mature: floor at ~0.3 t/person (cultural goods, biological samples, speciality equipment)

reference_design_human.tunnel_profile: 12 km at 3g sustained (linear to 852 m/s) + 18 km curvilinear (3–7g centripetal, 7g <3 s, coils maintain velocity only)

bottleneck_variables_for_sensitivity: exit_altitude_km (sets aero delta-v budget — exponential leverage via density)
vehicle_dry_fraction (exponential sensitivity on propellant and energy — the cliff)
vehicle_W_L_and_C_L (landing wing loading and hypersonic lift coefficient — Δv_aero = ρ₀v₀HC_Lg/(2RW_L), mass cancels)
complexity_per_resident_t (two-stage decay: starts at 1.5, floor at ~0.3)
prompt_for_external_model: You are given bridge-logistics-model-v3.json. The architecture has three layers of free delta-v: (1) electromagnetic launcher eliminates ~1.3–1.5 km/s of losses, (2) aerodynamic bank of lifting body converts surplus vertical to horizontal at zero propellant cost (closed-form: Δv_aero = ρ₀v₀HC_Lg/(2RW_L) — absolute mass cancels via wing-loading constraint), (3) vacuum-optimised engine burns near-horizontal. Reference design: 20 km exit (8 km summit + 12 km pylon, coasting — no coils in pylon). One ~30 km shared bore (12 km linear + 18 km curvilinear). CARGO: 20+g sustained throughout, up to 3 km/s exit, banks at full C_L (~500 m/s free, 20g peak, engine 7.2 km/s, 64% savings). HUMAN: 3g linear to 852 m/s, 5-7g centripetal in curve, 700 m/s exit (152 m/s gravity loss in 16 s pylon coast), banks at full C_L (~105 m/s free, ~1g peak, engine 7.8 km/s, 58% savings). One tunnel, one pylon, two programs. Pylon is pure coasting section shared by both. Same pylon, different exit velocity and tunnel geometry. Compute: (a) Δv_aero for any altitude, velocity, and g-limit; (b) engine duty and propellant via Tsiolkovsky; (c) habitat capacity from lunar export; (d) Earth complexity export via two-stage decay C(t)=max(1.5e^(-0.03t), 0.3); (e) launch tube requirements. Optimise for minimum propellant subject to pylon height and g-load constraints.
